skill.name: citation-chasing-mapping
skill.description: Trace citation networks using the Semantic Scholar API to discover related research. Supports automatic DOI, title, and PubMed ID lookup with multi-hop citation tracing. Builds knowledge graphs of papers, identifies seminal works by citation count and centrality, and exports structured citation data.
skill.summary: Automated citation network mapping for research discovery, literature review, and bibliometric analysis.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Evaluated 128 static findings from 5 files (1218 lines). All findings are false positives. The 37 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags refer to no actual crypto operations in the code. The 50+ 'system reconnaissance' flags are metadata field access from API response dictionaries. The 38 'shell backtick execution' flags are documentation code examples in Markdown. The tool is a legitimate academic citation analysis tool making authorized HTTPS requests to Semantic Scholar API and writing output files to user-specified paths. No credential access, obfuscation, or prompt injection was detected.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm pattern (false positive - dismissed)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 37 locations for weak crypto algorithm usage. No cryptographic operations exist in the codebase. The scanner likely matched variable names or standard library patterns incorrectly. The tool makes standard HTTPS requests via urllib.request to api.semanticscholar.org and does not implement any cryptographic functions.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance pattern (false positive - dismissed)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 50+ locations for 'system reconnaissance'. All instances are Python dictionary access patterns retrieving paper metadata fields (paperId, title, authors, year, citationCount) from Semantic Scholar API responses. This is standard data extraction from an academic API, not system reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Shell backtick execution (false positive - dismissed)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 38 locations for Ruby/shell backtick execution. All instances are Markdown documentation code blocks (fenced or inline) showing example commands like 'python scripts/main.py --help'. These are documentation examples, not dynamic shell execution. No command injection vectors exist.

low_findings.title: Certificate/key files (false positive - dismissed)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged lines 202 and 548 as certificate/key files. Line 202 is the method name 'identify_key_papers' referring to academically influential papers, not cryptographic keys. Line 548 is Python dict 'papers.keys()' method call. Neither involves any certificate or key material.

low_findings.title: Obfuscation heuristic (false positive - dismissed)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged obfuscation patterns including 'multiple bracket chains' and 'dangerous combination'. The code is clean, well-structured Python with clear comments. No obfuscation, encoded strings, or suspicious patterns exist. The heuristic likely triggered on the legitimate combination of network requests and file output common to academic analysis tools. No credentials or authentication tokens are accessed.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL (false positive - dismissed)
low_findings.description: The BASE_URL constant at line 40 points to the public Semantic Scholar API endpoint (api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1), a legitimate academic research API. Line 564 is a help text reference to arrows.app, a public graph visualization tool. Both are appropriate for an academic citation analysis tool.

content.user_title: Trace and map citation networks for research discovery
content.value_statement: Researchers spend hours manually tracking citations to understand how ideas connect across papers. This skill automates citation network construction, identifying seminal works and hidden research connections through multi-hop citation tracing.
actual_capabilities: Build citation networks from seed papers using Semantic Scholar API with configurable depth
Identify highly influential papers through in-network citation counts and centrality metrics
Trace citation relationships both forward (citing papers) and backward (references) across multiple hops
Export citation networks as structured knowledge graphs in JSON format
Search for papers by DOI, PubMed ID, or title keywords with automatic API lookups
Run in demo mode with example CRISPR citation data for testing and learning
limitations: Requires internet access to query the Semantic Scholar API for citation data
Limited by Semantic Scholar API rate limits and database coverage
Cannot access papers behind paywalls or in academic databases not indexed by Semantic Scholar
Citation depth beyond 3 hops may produce incomplete networks due to API coverage gaps

use_cases.title: Map foundational literature for a research chapter
use_cases.description: A PhD student studying immunotherapy starts with a known seed paper and builds a citation network to identify seminal works, key authors, and research clusters for their literature review.

use_cases.title: Support systematic review with citation tracking
use_cases.description: A medical librarian supporting an evidence synthesis project uses citation chasing to find additional relevant studies beyond database searches, ensuring comprehensive coverage.
use_cases.target_user: Medical librarian or systematic review specialist

use_cases.title: Analyze collaboration patterns in a research field
use_cases.description: A research director analyzes which institutions and authors collaborate most frequently in precision medicine by mapping co-citation networks and institutional affiliations.

prompt_templates.title: Basic citation lookup and network build
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a citation network starting from the paper with DOI 10.1038/nature14236. Use depth 2 and show me the most cited papers in the network.
prompt_templates.scenario: A researcher wants to quickly explore the citation landscape around a known paper.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-hop citation tracing from a PubMed ID
prompt_templates.prompt: Trace the citation network for PubMed ID 27669175 with depth 3. Limit to 20 papers per level and export the result to my-network.json.
prompt_templates.scenario: A systematic reviewer needs deeper citation tracing with file export.

prompt_templates.title: Title-based search with custom analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for 'deep learning for protein structure prediction', use the top result as seed, build a depth 2 network, and identify the top 10 seminal papers with their citation counts.
prompt_templates.scenario: A researcher has a topic but no specific seed paper, needing discovery from a title.

prompt_templates.title: Full workflow with offline network load
prompt_templates.prompt: Load the citation network from network.json, report the network summary statistics, and export the knowledge graph for visualization tools like Cytoscape.
prompt_templates.scenario: An experienced user reloading a previously saved network for further analysis.

output_examples.input: User provides a DOI: 10.1038/nature14236 with depth 2
output_examples.output: The tool returns a citation network summary including total papers found, citation relationships mapped, a ranked list of the most cited papers in the network with titles and years, and a knowledge graph JSON file saved to disk.

output_examples.input: User searches by title: 'CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing'
output_examples.output: The tool searches Semantic Scholar, presents the top matching papers with titles and publication years, selects the best match, builds the citation network at the configured depth, and displays a structured summary with network statistics and key papers.

best_practices: Start with highly-cited seed papers to build richer, more connected citation networks for analysis
Limit citation depth to 2 or 3 hops during initial exploration to avoid excessive API calls and network noise
Validate key findings by cross-referencing with multiple academic databases beyond Semantic Scholar
anti_patterns: Using incomplete or malformed DOIs and PubMed IDs which will cause API lookup failures
Setting depth higher than 3 without first exploring at lower depth to understand network size
Relying on single-source citation data as complete without acknowledging database coverage gaps

faq.question: What citation database does this tool use?
faq.answer: The tool uses the Semantic Scholar API, a free academic research database providing citation data for millions of papers across all scientific fields.

faq.question: How deep can the citation network go?
faq.answer: Depth 2 to 3 is recommended. Higher depths may produce incomplete networks due to API rate limits and gaps in Semantic Scholar database coverage.

faq.question: Can I use this for non-academic research?
faq.answer: The tool works with any content indexed by Semantic Scholar, including preprints, conference papers, and technical reports. Coverage of non-academic sources may be limited.

faq.question: What input formats are supported?
faq.answer: The tool supports DOI (starting with 10.), PubMed ID (numeric), paper title search, and offline JSON network files for previously saved data.

faq.question: How long does a typical network build take?
faq.answer: Build time depends on depth and max papers per level. A depth 2 search with 20 papers per level typically completes within 30 seconds, including built-in rate limiting delays.

faq.question: Can I save and reload citation networks?
faq.answer: Yes. The tool exports networks as JSON knowledge graph files. Use the --network-file option to reload previously saved networks for further analysis without additional API calls.
